data.title: Gloom, Boom and Doom author recommends buying gold but not bitcoin
data.description:
By Jules Rimmer

Marc Faber says yields will soon break out, probably higher

Marc Faber says rates will break out soon, and probably upward.

The economist once known as Dr. Doom, after forewarning the Japanese crash of the 1990s, said interest rates are likely to break higher soon, taking the stock market with it.

"This year, we'll get the big breakout of interest rates, either up or down," Marc Faber told Maggie Lake, in an interview on her "Talking Markets" show on YouTube Thursday. "And the stock market will not like it."

Bonds have essentially moved sideways since the pandemic, he notes, but if U.S. 10-year yields BX:TMUBMUSD10Y are offering roughly 4%, he believes this is no protection against real inflation that he calculates is rising by somewhere between 6% and 12% annually.

The Labor Department's official consumer price index rose 2.7% in the 12 months to November.

Gold (GC00), says the author of the "Gloom Boom Doom" report, is the store of value that nobody owns enough of, the reciprocal of the paper money that he despises and what rallies when currencies fall.

His championing of gold is no surprise to those who've been following his weekly newsletter since inception in 1990 or anyone who read his book, "Tomorrow's Gold, Age of Discovery" when it was published in 2001.

Faber likes to measure everything in gold, with the result that from this perspective, in general terms almost all assets over the last 20 to 40 years have gone down.

Considering another putative store of value - bitcoin (BTCUSD) - Faber expresses reservations. "When we have the next war, they will switch off the internet... and the mess will be complete," he says.

One of the reasons why Faber adopted such a bearish stance on the Japanese market in the 80s and 90s was because of its disproportionate representation in global indices. Japan peaked at about 50% in 1989. It's easy to guess how he views the massive 65% weighting U.S. stocks SPX now have in the global index.

Central banks, he argues, have lost control of interest rates and he remarks that while the Fed may be able to keep the short end of the yield curve subdued and Trump may succeed in his dream of getting Fed funds (FF00) to 1%, the long end of the curve is not reacting favorably. Trump is an "ignorant interventionist" and a "big money-spender," according to Faber, so he thinks the U.S. economy will sour as a consequence.

While gold, silver (SI00) and platinum (PL00) are his preferred exposure, Faber also loves cashflow and adds decent-yielding dividend stocks to his list of desirable investments. U.S. tech stocks, however, do not make the cut and owing to their expensive valuations, he predicts rotation out of the Magnificent 7 MAGS grouping of stocks or U.S. stocks in general into the rest of the world.

Faber is positive about one thing: the ability of capitalism to lift the world's populace out of poverty.

And another: he's almost 80 years old and he said he rides motorbikes around northern Thailand where he lives. That demands some optimism too.

-Jules Rimmer

data.title: UniQure Climbs on Scheduling FDA Meeting for Huntington's Disease Treatment
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Shares of UniQure were higher after the gene therapy company said it had scheduled a Type A meeting with the Food and Drug Administration to discuss a Biologics License Application for the company's investigational Huntington's disease treatment.

The stock was up 17% at $26.99 Friday morning. The stock has nearly doubled in value in the past year.

The company said Friday that the meeting would discuss accelerated approval of the treatment, known as AMT-130.

"We look forward to a constructive discussion with the FDA as we work toward a timely resolution regarding an accelerated approval pathway for AMT-130," Chief Executive Officer Matt Kapusta said.

"The Huntington's disease community, including patients and clinicians, has emphasized the profound unmet medical need and the importance of timely access to potentially disease-modifying therapies such as AMT-130," Kapusta added. We remain deeply committed to patient access while continuing to collaborate closely with the FDA."

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: TSMC Stock Rises After Posting Double-Digit December Growth
data.description:
December revenue of NT$335 billion put the spotlight on Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. as demand trends stayed firm late in the year.

TSM said consolidated revenue for December 2025 reached about NT$335.0 billion, up 20% from a year earlier but down 2.5% from November, according to a Friday filing. U.S.-listed shares edged up about 1% in premarket trading after the update.

For full-year 2025, revenue climbed to NT$3.81 trillion, a 32% increase from 2024. The result reflected continued strength across advanced chips tied to artificial intelligence, high-performance computing, and data center demand.

The filing also outlined financial ties with subsidiaries. TSMC Nanjing and TSMC Washington held outstanding loans of roughly NT$15.3 billion and NT$1.9 billion, respectively, at year end. The company also carried guarantees for units in North America, Global operations, and Arizona as it expanded manufacturing capacity overseas.

TSM also disclosed derivative positions. Forward contracts that did not apply hedge accounting showed unrealized losses at the end of December, alongside realized losses on contracts that expired during the year.

All in all, the 2025 growth was enhanced and investors could have a better insight into the balance sheet as TSM continues to expand across the globe.

data.title: US Consumer Sentiment Rises Slightly in January
data.description:
The University of Michigan’s consumer sentiment index inched up for the second consecutive month, rising to 54.0 in January 2026, its highest level since September 2025 and slightly above market expectations of 53.5, according to a preliminary estimate.

Gains were concentrated among lower-income consumers, while sentiment among higher-income households slipped.

Overall, US households reported modest improvement in economic perceptions over the past two months, but sentiment remains nearly 25% below January 2025 levels.

Consumers continue to worry about high prices and a softening labor market, though concerns about tariffs appear to be gradually easing.

Year-ahead inflation expectations held steady at 4.2%, the lowest since January 2025, yet still well above the 3.3% recorded a year ago.

Meanwhile, long-term inflation expectations ticked up slightly, rising to 3.4% from 3.2% in December.

data.title: Bitcoin price clings to $90K as traders eye US Supreme Court tariff ruling
data.description:
Bitcoin hovered at $90,000 around Friday’s Wall Street open as markets braced for US trade tariff news.

Key points:

  • RIsk-asset traders await news over US trade tariffs, as bets see the Supreme Court striking down the measures.

  • US unemployment data misses expectations, with the Federal Reserve seen holding rates this month.

  • Bitcoin traders demand clearer signals to end the rangebound price deadlock.

US tariff ruling could come Friday

Data from TradingView showed indecisive BTC price action ahead of a potential Supreme Court ruling over the tariffs, known as a risk-asset volatility catalyst.

Commenting, trading resource The Kobeissi Letter noted that bets favored President Donald Trump’s trade measures being ruled illegal.

The Kobeissi Letter
@KobeissiLetter

BREAKING: The US Supreme Court's ruling on President Trump's tariffs is expected as soon as today, with a 74% chance of tariffs being ruled illegal, per Polymarket.

Trump has asked Americans to "pray" that the Supreme Court does not rule his tariffs to be illegal. pic.twitter.com/ei2iTSVjJY

Jan 09, 2026

“The decision could reshape trade policy and ripple across global markets, including crypto,” crypto education platform Coin Bureau added in a post on X.

The forthcoming news meant that US unemployment data, which came in lower than expected, took a backseat.

As Cointelegraph reported, the Federal Reserve was already expected to hold interest rates at current levels at its January meeting.

“The Fed is set to pause interest rate cuts,” Kobeissi confirmed on the back of the data.

Bitcoin price inertia keeps traders away

Among traders, remained a “no trade” pair amid a lack of a clear trend.

“Still ranging as has been the case for the past ~2 months. I am not interested in trying to trade the next 5% move on this at all,” Daan Crypto Trades told X followers on the day. 

“Just chilling until the start of the year chop is over and we see a decisive breakout to either side.”

An accompanying chart showed price interacted with its 200-period moving average (MA) cloud on four-hour timeframes.

Trading account Deadline flagged $88,000 and $92,000 as key points of interest while referencing an unfilled “gap” in CME Group’s Bitcoin futures market from the new year.

Deadline☠️🐧
@cryptodeadline

$BTC is currently in a no trading zone.

Either Bitcoin needs to reclaim the $92,000 level to prove the bulls are back, or it'll likely drop towards the $88,000 zone to fill that remaining CME gap.

Patience is the only play right now. pic.twitter.com/wfL0Gfo5NK

Jan 09, 2026

Little tricky market circumstances here, as Bitcoin is correcting again,” crypto trader, analyst and entrepreneur Michaël van de Poppe added.

“However, holding the 21-Day MA would be great, as that would prevent a further cascade.”

data.title: China to scrap export tax rebates for photovoltaic and battery products
data.description:
China will cancel value-added tax export rebates for photovoltaic products from April 1, the finance ministry said in a joint statement with the State Taxation Administration on Friday.

The ministry also said VAT export rebates for battery products will be cut to 6% from 9% between April and December and scrapped entirely from January 1, 2027.

Consumption-tax rebate rules for these products will remain unchanged, the statement added.

The China Photovoltaic Industry Association, in a statement later on Friday, said the move should help curb an excessive decline of export prices in the long run as China's PV products face intensifying competition in overseas markets.

It noted that some Chinese exporters were using rebates as a price discount for foreign buyers.

"Timely reduction or cancellation of export rebates for photovoltaic products can help promote a rational return of foreign market prices and reduce the risk of trade frictions," the association said.

China previously reduced the export tax rebate for PV products to 9% from 13% in December 2024 as part of its broader efforts to curb overcapacity and deflationary price wars amid international trade tensions.

data.title: TSMC Stock Rises After Bumper Sales. What That Says About a Potential AI Bubble. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Adam Clark

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing was gaining early Friday after better-than-expected sales figures helped dispel concerns about a bubble in artificial-intelligence spending.

TSMC reported December revenue of 335.00 billion New Taiwan dollars ($10.60 billion), up 20% from the same month the previous year. That brought the Taiwanese chip manufacturer's fourth-quarter sales overall to 1.046 trillion New Taiwan dollars, beating expectations of $1.018 trillion New Taiwan dollars, according to a FactSet poll of analysts' expectations.

American depositary receipts of TSMC were up 0.8% in premarket trading, having gained 53% over the past 12 months. U.S.-listed shares of Dutch chipmaking equipment company ASML Holding --which sells machines to TSMC-- were up 4%.

TSMC is the main supplier of chips to Nvidia — the leader of semiconductors used for AI applications. TSMC also makes the core processors inside Apple iPhones, Qualcomm mobile chipsets, and processors made by Advanced Micro Devices.

TSMC doesn't issue commentary alongside its monthly report and will report its full fourth-quarter earnings on Jan. 15, but all the signs are that AI demand continues to be strong.

The company previously raised its guidance for revenue growth in 2025 to the middle range of 30%-40%, from about 30% in U.S. dollar terms. The main driver of that growth is AI-related chip revenue, which TSMC has previously said it expects to have doubled in 2025 and grow at a mid-40% annual rate for the next five years.

Analysts at J.P. Morgan recently backed TSMC to keep more than a 95% market share in manufacturing 2-nanometer scale chips, representing the coming generation of advanced AI processors, despite efforts by Intel to break its dominance.

Write to Adam Clark at adam.clark@barrons.com

As at close of business on 8 January 2026, the unaudited net asset value per share calculated in accordance with the AIC formula (including current financial year revenue items and excluding shares held in treasury) was 199.7p and the net asset value per share with debt marked at fair value was 200.6p. As the Company's shares are now ex-dividend, the dividend has been deducted from the net asset value.

As at close of business on 8 January 2026, the unaudited net asset value per share (excluding current financial year revenue items and shares held in treasury) was 199.2p and the net asset value per share with debt marked at fair value was 200.1p.

Debt marked at fair value is the Company's estimate of the "fair value" of its senior unsecured notes. The current estimated fair value of the Company's senior unsecured notes is based on a discount rate calculated using the redemption yield of the relevant existing UK Gilt plus a credit spread determined by the month-end difference in redemption yields between the ICE BofAML 5-10 Year A Sterling Non-Gilt Index and the ICE BofAML 5-10 Year UK Gilt Index. The discount rate is calculated and updated monthly and applied daily to determine the Company's published fair value NAVs.
